what: what are get post put delete
why: why use put instead of post
how: how to make api calls in javascript
how to update data on a server

1_beginner_explanation.summary: An **API Call** is when your frontend JavaScript asks a backend server to do something with data. Most APIs use the REST pattern, mapping actions to HTTP Methods: **GET** (read data), **POST** (create new data), **PUT/PATCH** (update existing data), and **DELETE** (remove data). This matches the standard CRUD operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete) of a database.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: Making an API call is like interacting with a librarian. GET is asking 'Can I read the book on JavaScript?'. POST is handing them a brand new manuscript saying 'Please add this to the library'. DELETE is saying 'Please permanently remove book #123'. The librarian (API) handles the complex filing rules so you don't have to.

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: By default, `fetch('url')` sends a GET request. To use other methods, you must pass an options object as the second parameter. POST/PUT/PATCH requests usually require a `body` (the data you are sending) and a `Content-Type` header (telling the server the data is JSON).
2_internal_mechanism.memory: No special JS memory structures, but understanding the mapping of HTTP Verbs to backend database operations is crucial for full-stack engineering.

3_real_world_example.use_case: A complete CRUD cycle: getting, creating, and deleting a task.

4_common_confusion.trap: Trying to send a `body` with a `GET` request. The HTTP standard strictly forbids GET requests from having bodies. If you need to send data to filter a GET request, you must put it in the URL as a query string (e.g., `/api/search?q=apple`).
4_common_confusion.fix: Data goes in the URL for GET. Data goes in the `body` for POST/PUT.

6_edge_case.quirk: The difference between PUT and PATCH: PUT is meant to completely replace a resource (send the whole object). PATCH is meant to partially update a resource (send only the fields that changed).
